column1:
Which of the following anatomic variants of ICA anatomy is most often associated with symptoms of cerebral ischemia?

A: tortuosity
B: duplication
C: kinking
D: coiling
column2:
Answer: C. kinking

Feedback:
Kinking - vessel takes a sharp turn of 90 degrees or less, mimics stenosis and is related to plaque/stenosis formation. It is the
anatomic variant most commonly associated with symptoms of ischemia
Coiling - vessel forms a circle during its course
Tortuosity - vessel forms an S-shaped curve

column1:
Which of the following is not normally a branch of the ophthalmic artery?

A: frontal artery
B: supraorbital artery
C: nasal artery
D: posterior auricular artery
column2:
Answer: D. posterior auricular artery

Feedback:
The posterior auricular artery branches from the ECA and supplies the ear with oxygenated blood.

column1:
What are the standard windows used for a complete transcranial Doppler exam?

A: suboccipital, transtemporal, transorbital, and submandibular windows
B: suboccipital, transtemporal, and periorbital windows
C: suboccipital, transtemporal windows
D: transtemporal window only, other views performed if abnormalities identified
column2:
Answer: A. suboccipital, transtemporal, transorbital, and submandibular windows

Feedback:
The four standard windows used for a TCD exam are the suboccipital, transtemporal, transorbital/ophthalmic, and
submandibular windows. The periorbital window is a supplemental view used to assess the frontal artery (branch of the
ophthalmic artery).

column1:
The highest velocity in the normal common carotid artery is usually identified:

A: <1cm from the bulb
B: < 3cm from the bulb
C: in the first 3cm of the vessel
D: mid CCA
column2:
Answer: C. in the first 3cm of the vessel

Feedback:
The highest ICA velocity is usually identified in the first 3cm of the vessel. The highest CCA velocity is also usually identified in the
proximal artery.

column1:
The image provided was taken during a routine carotid examination. Which of the following statements is true regarding the images?

A: This is a normal vertebral artery
B: This is a mildly stenosed ICA
C: This is a normal ICA
D: This is a normal ECA
column2:
Answer: D. This is a normal ECA

Feedback:
Note the branch originating from the proximal vessel. The ICA has no branches identified extracranially. The vertebral arteries
course through the pedicles of the vertebra and this vessel is demonstrated anterior to the spine.

column1:
Using the transtemporal window, the normal middle cerebral artery ______________________.

A: will demonstrate flow toward the probe
B: will demonstrate flow away from the probe
C: will demonstrate biphasic flow
D: will demonstrate a clear spectral window
column2:
Answer: A. will demonstrate flow toward the probe

Feedback:
Using the transtemporal window, the normal MCA will demonstrate monophasic flow toward the probe. Because the
intracranial vessels are so small, no spectral window is demonstrated on the waveform.

column1:
Which of the following structures is supplied by branches of the internal carotid artery?

A: lateral nose only
B: parietal lobe only
C: eyes and lateral nose, but not the parietal lobe
D: eyes, lateral nose and parietal lobe
column2:
Answer: D. eyes, lateral nose and parietal lobe

Feedback:
Branches of the internal carotid artery supply the eyes, lateral nose and parietal lobe.

column1:
When scanning the internal carotid, which way would you angle your probe to locate the external carotid artery in most patients?

A: 40 degrees medially
B: superior and/or lateral
C: medial and/or anterior
D: lateral and/or anterior
column2:
Answer: C. medial and/or anterior

Feedback:
When scanning the internal carotid artery, you angle your probe medial and anterior to locate the external carotid artery in
most patients

column1:
What is demonstrated on the image?

A: normal internal jugular vein flow with reversed vertebral artery flow
B: normal carotid artery flow with vertebral artery flow reversal
C: normal carotid artery and vertebral artery flow
D: normal internal jugular vein flow with normal vertebral artery flow
column2:
Answer: C. normal carotid artery and vertebral artery flow

Feedback:
The carotid and vertebral arteries should normally demonstrate flow moving in the same direction. Because of the angle of the
color box, the blue color is demonstrating flow moving away from the transducer. In standard practice, the color box should be
angled the opposing direction or the heel toe technique used to demonstrate the vessels and flow moving toward the
transducer. Red color is most commonly used to demonstrate antegrade flow and blue is used for retrograde flow.

column1:
Which artery supplies the brainstem with arterial blood?

A: anterior cerebral artery
B: basilar artery
C: posterior cerebral artery
D: posterior communicating artery
column2:
Answer: B. basilar artery

Feedback:
Branches from the vertebral and basilar arteries feed the cerebellum and portions of the brain stem.

column1:
In the Circle of Willis there is/are:

A: 2 anterior communicator arteries and 1 middle communicator artery
B: 1 anterior communicator artery and 2 middle communicator arteries
C: 1 anterior communicator artery and 2 posterior communicator arteries
D: 2 anterior communicator arteries and 1 posterior communicator artery
column2:
Answer: C. 1 anterior communicator artery and 2 posterior communicator arteries

Feedback:
In the Circle of Willis there is 1 anterior communicator artery and 2 posterior communicator arteries.

column1:
Which of the following describes the probe placement for a Doppler evaluation of the frontal artery?

A: near the inner canthus of the eye
B: approximately 2cm above the zygomatic arch
C: just above the mid-point of the eyebrow
D: near the outer canthus of the eye
column2:
Answer: A. near the inner canthus of the eye

Feedback:
Periorbital Doppler is a CW Doppler evaluation of the frontal artery (branch of the ophthalmic artery). An 8-10MHz pencil probe
is placed near the inner canthus of the eye. Normal flow direction will be toward the probe.

column1:
Letter B indicates which of the following structures?

A: Superficial temporal artery
B: Thyrocervical trunk
C: Vertebral artery
D: Internal thoracic artery
column2:
Answer: C. Vertebral artery

Feedback:
A. Internal thoracic artery
B. Vertebral artery
C. Thyrocervical trunk
D. Right CCA

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the vertebral arteries?

A: The right vertebral artery is normally smaller than the left.
B: The right vertebral artery is the first branch of the aortic arch.
C: Vertebral artery flow is normally biphasic due to turbulence at their junction with the basilar artery.
D: The right and left vertebral arteries empty their blood into the right and left basilar arteries.
column2:
Answer: A. The right vertebral artery is normally smaller than the left.

Feedback:
The right vertebral artery is normally smaller than the left vertebral artery. It originates from the right subclavian artery. The
right and left vertebral arteries merge distally to form a single basilar artery. Normal Doppler evaluation demonstrates a low
resistance, monophasic waveform.

column1:
Letter C indicates which of the following structures?

A: Thyrocervical trunk
B: Superficial temporal artery
C: Internal thoracic artery
D: Vertebral artery
column2:
Answer: A. Thyrocervical trunk

Feedback:
A. Internal thoracic artery
B. Vertebral artery
C. Thyrocervical trunk
D. Right CCA

column1:
Which of the following Doppler imaging characteristics will not assist when trying to identify a vessel on a TCD exam?

A: Velocity of flow
B: Sample size
C: Sample location/depth
D: Direction of flow
column2:
Answer: B. Sample size

Feedback:
TCD exams rely on range resolution to determine the vessels interrogated on the exam. The depth of the sample, flow velocity
and direction of flow detected are used to determine the vessel being interrogated by PW Doppler. The sample size will only
affect the amount of flow sampled and is not useful for determining the vessel being evaluated.

column1:
Which of the following arteries will be compressed when evaluating frontal artery flow for a periorbital Doppler exam?

A: internal carotid artery and vertebral artery
B: external carotid artery and vertebral artery
C: superficial temporal artery and infraorbital artery
D: ophthalmic artery and contralateral common carotid artery
column2:
Answer: C. superficial temporal artery and infraorbital artery

Feedback:
A periorbital Doppler exam involves using CW Doppler to evaluate flow in the frontal artery (branch of the ophthalmic artery).
Normal flow direction will be toward the probe. Flow moving away from the probe indicates a significant obstruction in the
ipsilateral ICA. Compression of the infraorbital, facial and superficial temporal arteries is performed on both sides of the head,
while monitoring the frontal artery flow. If compression of one of these arteries causes decreased or reversed flow in the frontal
artery, the compressed vessel is being used as a collateral to fill the frontal artery (not the ICA).

column1:
Which of the following arteries is supplied with blood from the ECA?

A: maxillary artery
B: posterior communicator artery
C: ophthalmic artery
D: anterior choroidal artery
column2:
Answer: A. maxillary artery

Feedback:
The maxillary artery is a branch of the ECA. The other choices are fed by the ICA.

column1:
When evaluating the carotid siphon from the submandibular window, the sample depth should be set to value between:

A: 30-50mm
B: 40-60mm
C: 60-80mm
D: 80-110mm
column2:
Answer: C. 60-80mm

Feedback:
The carotid siphon is evaluated when the sample depth is placed at an approximate depth of 70mm (between 60 and 80mm).

column1:
Branches of the ___________________ connect to branches of the vertebral arteries to allow collateral flow from the anterior circulation to the posterior circulation.

A: basilar artery
B: ophthalmic artery
C: subclavian artery
D: occipital artery
column2:
Answer: D. occipital artery

Feedback:
The occipital artery is a branch of the ECA. Branches of the occipital artery meet branches of the vertebral artery. This is a
possible collateral pathway for obstruction in the vertebral system or the ICA system on the same side.
If vertebral branches meet basilar branches, this would be posterior circulation connected to posterior circulation.
The ophthalmic artery is in the anterior aspect of the brain. Branching would not meet those of the posterior brain.
The subclavian artery is in the upper chest.

column1:
Which of the following describes a cardiovascular application of the Bernoulli Principle?

A: explains the relationship of vessel radius and flow volume
B: explains the reason for aliasing of the Doppler signal at high velocities
C: explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb
D: explains the reason for increased resistance to flow at an area of focal stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb

Feedback:
Bernoulli Principle explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb.

column1:
The blue arrow indicates which of the following structures?

A: Ophthalmic
B: Thyrocervical trunk
C: Internal thoracic artery
D: Superior thyroidal artery
column2:
Answer: D. Superior thyroidal artery

Feedback:
The superior thyroidal artery is the first branch of the ECA. Compare the size of the two vessels imaged and their anatomic
location. The smaller, more medial vessel demonstrates the branch coursing inferiorly into the neck to the thyroid.

column1:
Which of the following is a potential collateral pathway for extracranial to intracranial blood flow?

A: Superficial temporal artery branches connect to small branches of the ascending pharyngeal artery
B: Ascending pharyngeal branches connect to small branches of the superior thyroidal artery
C: Ascending pharyngeal branches connect to small branches of the lingual artery
D: Facial and maxillary artery branches connect to small branches of the orbital artery
column2:
Answer: D. Facial and maxillary artery branches connect to small branches of the orbital artery

Feedback:
ECA branches provide collateral pathways when stenosis is present in the intracranial system. The superficial temporal artery
branches (ECA) connect to small branches of the ophthalmic artery (ICA). Facial and maxillary artery branches (ECA) connect to
small branches of the orbital artery (ICA). Ascending pharyngeal branches (ECA) connect to small branches of the vertebral
artery (posterior circulation). The other answer choices given describe connection of two vessels in the extracranial system,
which would not provide flow to intracranial vessels.
Tip for collateralization questions: Know your branches! For this question, the key is selecting branches from two different
supply lines. ECA and posterior circulation branches that are connected to the intracranial branches will supply flow to a Circle
of Willis that is obstructed in an area.

column1:
Which of the following describes the proper technique for evaluating the MCA through the temporal window using TCD?

A: Midline probe placement, slight anterior angulation with 70mm depth
B: Sharp anterior angulation with 65mm depth
C: Midline probe placement, slight anterior angulation with 50mm depth
D: Sharp anterior probe angulation, 50mm depth
column2:
Answer: C. Midline probe placement, slight anterior angulation with 50mm depth

Feedback:
The MCA is best evaluated through the temporal window. The probe is placed midline on the side of the cranium. The MCA
should be identified at an average depth of 50mm. Slight anterior angulation may be required.

column1:
During a TCD exam the depth is set to 100mm while using the suboccipital window. What vessel will be interrogated and what direction will the flow be moving?

A: basilar, toward the transducer
B: carotid siphon, varies with portion of the siphon evaluated
C: vertebral, toward the transducer
D: basilar, away from the transducer
column2:
Answer: D. basilar, away from the transducer

Feedback:
60 - 90mm suboccipital window = vertebral
80 - 120mm suboccipital window = basilar
Both vessels will normally exhibit flow moving away from the transducer and toward the brain

column1:
Which statement below is true regarding the normal CCA Doppler tracing?

A: The waveform is a combination of external and internal carotid characteristics with high resistance peaks and increased diastolic flow.
B: The waveform exhibits a sharp upstroke and minimal diastolic flow velocities.
C: The waveform exhibits a triphasic pattern of flow.
D: The waveform exhibits a delayed upstroke and sharp diastolic downslope.
column2:
Answer: A. The waveform is a combination of external and internal carotid characteristics with high resistance peaks and increased diastolic flow.

Feedback:
The waveform is a combination of external and internal carotid characteristics with high resistance peaks and increased
diastolic flow. The waveform demonstrates a steep upslope which is indicative of a higher resistance waveform. The gradual
downslope that never touches the baseline indicates lower resistance in diastole. The waveform is monophasic with one peak
above the baseline.

column1:
The most common normal variant in the external/internal carotid artery anatomy is:

A: the ECA is absent and the ICA has branches that feed the extracranial structures
B: the ECA branches directly from the ICA at the level of the angle of the mandible
C: the ECA lies posterior and medial to the ICA
D: the ECA lies posterior and lateral to the ICA
column2:
Answer: D. the ECA lies posterior and lateral to the ICA

Feedback:
In most patients the ECA is the anterior medial branch of CCA . There are several normal variants in ECA/ICA position, with the
most common being the ECA lies posterior and lateral to the ICA

column1:
The Doppler tracing is most consistent with a tracing obtained from which normal extracranial vessel?

A: internal carotid artery
B: common carotid artery
C: external carotid artery
D: middle cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: A. internal carotid artery

Feedback:
The ICA is normally low resistance, monophasic flow with good amounts of antegrade diastolic flow. The flow in the MCA would
be similar to this waveform but it is an intracranial vessel and does not demonstrate a spectral window due to the small caliber
of the vessel. The ECA and CCA waveforms are higher resistance with a sharper upstroke and peak.

column1:
Which of the following vessels is evaluated using the submandibular TCD window?

A: distal internal carotid artery
B: middle cerebral artery
C: anterior cerebral artery
D: vertebral arteries
column2:
Answer: A. distal internal carotid artery

Feedback:
The distal ICA can be evaluated using the submandibular approach.

column1:
Which of the following normally demonstrates a higher resistance Doppler flow profile?

A: external carotid artery
B: post-prandial superior mesenteric artery
C: renal artery
D: hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: A. external carotid artery

Feedback:
"Organ flow" will typically be low resistance and constant flow is needed for the organs to function. The post prandial SMA will
be lower resistance with increased diastolic flow due to increased digestive system function. The renal and hepatic arteries will
also have low resistance flow with increased diastolic flow because they constantly function. "Extremity flow" is typically high
resistance as muscles/tissue need much less inflow, especially at rest. The external carotid artery and its branches feed mainly
muscles and tissue, therefore the flow demonstrates a higher resistance waveform.

column1:
The most common congenital anomaly of the Circle of Willis is:

A: the absence of one or more communicating arteries
B: the duplication of one or more communicating arteries
C: the duplication of one or both of the middle cerebral arteries
D: the absence of one or both of the middle cerebral arteries
column2:
Answer: A. the absence of one or more communicating arteries

Feedback:
The most common congenital anomaly of the Circle of Willis is the absence of one or more communicating arteries.

column1:
Which of the following vessels is most commonly assessed for the presence of a bruit?

A: common carotid artery
B: dorsalis pedis artery
C: common femoral artery
D: subclavian artery
column2:
Answer: A. common carotid artery

Feedback:
The CCA is the artery that is most commonly assessed for a bruit. A bruit is an abnormal sound of turbulence that can indicate
stenosis. Tortuosity, vessel kinking and elevated blood pressure can be normal causes of a bruit.

column1:
The posterior communicating artery connects the:

A: right and left sides of the circle of willis
B: ECA to the ICA
C: right and left vertebral arteries
D: anterior and posterior cerebral vessels
column2:
Answer: D. anterior and posterior cerebral vessels

Feedback:
The posterior communicators connect the posterior cerebral circulation to the anterior cerebral circulation (PCA and the MCA).
The ACA and MCA are part of the anterior circulation. The PCA and basilar/vertebral arteries are part of the posterior circulation.

column1:
The supraorbital artery originates from the ophthalmic artery and joins what branch of the ECA?

A: superficial temporal artery
B: posterior auricular artery
C: lingual artery
D: vertebral artery
column2:
Answer: A. superficial temporal artery

Feedback:
The supraorbital artery originates from the ophthalmic artery and joins the superficial temporal artery(branch of the ECA). This
arrangement allows for collateral flow to occur on the same side of the body if the ICA is obstructed.

column1:
Which of the following originates first from the ascending aorta?

A: subclavian artery
B: left carotid artery
C: coronary arteries
D: innominate artery
column2:
Answer: C. coronary arteries

Feedback:
Keep in mind that the question asks for the first branch of the ascending aorta, not the arch. The coronary arteries originate just
above the aortic valve in the heart.

column1:
What artery courses cephalad on the anterior right side of the trachea to bifurcate at the level of the sternoclavicular joint?

A: carotid artery
B: subclavian artery
C: innominate artery
D: vertebral artery
column2:
Answer: C. innominate artery

Feedback:
There is only one innominate (brachiocephalic) artery on the right side of the body. It originates from the aortic arch and travels
cephalad in a position that is to the right and anterior to the trachea. It bifurcates into the right subclavian and vertebral
arteries.

column1:
The ECA is normally ______________ to the ICA.

A: Superior
B: Lateral
C: Medial
D: Inferior
column2:
Answer: C. Medial

Feedback:
The ECA usually courses anteriorly and medially in the neck to feed structures including the face, tongue and scalp. The ICA
usually courses posteriorly and laterally to enter the skull and feed the cerebral tissues.

column1:
Which of the following is the best patient position for performing a transcranial Doppler exam to obtain a waveform from the basilar artery?

A: supine with chin in neutral position
B: seated on a stool, with their back to the Sonographer and the machine
C: left lateral decubitus with left hand placed under the head for support
D: seated on a stool, facing the Sonographer and the machine
column2:
Answer: C. left lateral decubitus with left hand placed under the head for support

Feedback:
The patient needs to be able to hold their head steady for the exam. Lying down with the head supported will provide the best
position for the exam. Placing the hand under the head will also help to raise it from the stretcher to maintain a straight neck.

column1:
Which transcranial window is most commonly used to evaluate the carotid siphon and the ophthalmic artery?

A: temporal
B: submandibular
C: occipital
D: orbital
column2:
Answer: D. orbital

Feedback:
The orbital or ophthalmic window is used to evaluate the carotid siphon and the ophthalmic artery. If the siphon cannot be
evaluated on this view, the supplemental submandibular approach may be used.

column1:
The _______________________ is described as the segment of the vessel located between the distal ICA and the origin of the anterior communicating artery.

A: A2 segment of the ACA
B: M3 segment of the MCA
C: A1 segment of the ACA
D: M1 segment of the MCA
column2:
Answer: C. A1 segment of the ACA

Feedback:
The A1 segment is located between the distal ICA and the origin of the anterior communicator artery. The A2 segment is located
distal to the origin of the anterior communicator artery.

column1:
Which of the following is an expected characteristic of a normal ICA?

A: Carries less than half of the blood to cerebrum
B: Has a low resistance waveform
C: Is often a rare site for plaque formation
D: Has 2 extracranial branches on each side
column2:
Answer: B. Has a low resistance waveform

Feedback:
The ICA has no extracranial branches and does deliver the majority of the blood flow to the brain. The ICA also has an increased
prevalence for atherosclerotic changes, when compared to the CCA and ECA. The brain requires constant blood flow throughout
the cardiac cycle. The cerebral structures normally offer very little resistance to the incoming flow of blood. The ICA should
normally demonstrate a low resistance waveform, while the ECA demonstrates a higher resistance waveform.

column1:
Which of the following describes the proper technique for evaluating the ACA through the temporal window?

A: Anterior angulation, 50mm depth
B: Anterior angulation with 65mm depth
C: Midline placement with 50mm depth
D: Posterior angulation with 65mm depth
column2:
Answer: B. Anterior angulation with 65mm depth

Feedback:
When using the temporal window, the ACA is evaluated with anterior probe angulation set at a 65mm depth.

column1:
What is occurring on the Doppler tracing where the arrows are pointing?

A: Patient is swallowing
B: Patient is snoring
C: Temporal tap
D: Atrial fibrillation
column2:
Answer: C. Temporal tap

Feedback:
The temporal tap is performed by lightly tapping the temporal artery located at the soft spot on the anterior aspect of the
temporal bone. The temporal artery is a branch of the ECA and the vibrations from the tapping will be seen within the ECA
waveform. The ICA does not communicate with the temporal artery and no response will be identified from the tapping
maneuver. This technique is used to differentiate the ECA from the ICA.

column1:
The carotid siphon:

A: usually produces a waveform with a clear spectral window
B: is an area of flow separation due to the increase in vessel diameter at the bulb
C: supplies blood to the ophthalmic artery
D: causes an area of decreased resistance to flow in the distal ICA
column2:
Answer: C. supplies blood to the ophthalmic artery

Feedback:
ICA Siphon - distal ICA segment that courses anteriorly then medially, then posteriorly forming an "S". The tortuous path leads
to reduced flow, increased resistance, and causes turbulence. There will be no spectral window visible due to the tortuous
course of the vessel. The siphon has increased risk for flow reducing lesions, such as atheroma and thrombus. The ophthalmic
artery branches from the carotid siphon.

column1:
At what level in the neck does the common carotid artery normally bifurcate?

A: At the upper border of the thyroid cartilage
B: At the lower border of the larynx
C: At the upper level of the thyrocervical trunk
D: At the angle of the mandible
column2:
Answer: A. At the upper border of the thyroid cartilage

Feedback:
The common carotid artery normally bifurcates at the level of the upper border of the thyroid cartilage.

column1:
The internal carotid artery terminates into which two branches of the Circle of Willis?

A: middle and anterior cerebral arteries
B: Anterior and posterior cerebral arteries
C: middle cerebral and posterior communicator arteries
D: Vertebral and basilar arteries
column2:
Answer: A. middle and anterior cerebral arteries

Feedback:
Bilateral ICAs course superiorly to join the Circle of Willis on the lateral aspect of each side of the brain. In most patients, they
terminate at the origination of the MCA and ACA. The PCA is a branch of the basilar artery. The PCoA is branch of the distal ICA.

column1:
Which of the following is supplied with arterial blood through branches of the ECA?

A: thyroid gland
B: eyes
C: cerebellum
D: brain stem
column2:
Answer: A. thyroid gland

Feedback:
The superior thyroidal artery is the first branch of the ECA. The eyes are supplied with blood through intracranial branches of
the ICA. The cerebellum and brain stem are supplied by the posterior circulation (vertebral > basilar > cerebellar).

column1:
Which of the following is a direct branch of the aortic arch?

A: right vertebral
B: left vertebral
C: right carotid
D: left carotid
column2:
Answer: D. left carotid

Feedback:
The three main branches of the aortic arch are the innominate artery, left common carotid artery and left subclavian artery

column1:
Which branch of the Circle of Willis supplies the corpus callosum and the cavum septum pellucidum with arterial blood?

A: anterior cerebral artery
B: posterior cerebral artery
C: middle cerebral artery
D: basilar artery
column2:
Answer: A. anterior cerebral artery

Feedback:
The corpus callosum and the cavum septum pellucidum are located along the midline in the anterior brain. Both structures are
fed by the anterior cerebral artery.

column1:
Which of the following are branches of the distal ICA?

A: middle cerebral artery and anterior communicating artery
B: middle, anterior and posterior cerebral artery
C: middle cerebral artery, anterior cerebral artery and posterior communicating artery
D: supraorbital artery and posterior cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: C. middle cerebral artery, anterior cerebral artery and posterior communicating artery

Feedback:
Ophthalmic artery is the first branch from siphon area inside the cranium and branches into nasal, frontal & supraorbital
arteries. The anterior choroidal artery and posterior communicating artery are the other two main intracranial branches of the
distal ICA before it terminates at the bifurcation into the ACA and MCA once it reaches the circle of willis.

column1:
The _______________________ is described as the segment of the vessel located between the distal basilar artery and the origin of the posterior communicator artery.

A: P2 segment of the PCA
B: M3 segment of the MCA
C: M1 segment of the MCA
D: P1 segment of the PCA
column2:
Answer: D. P1 segment of the PCA

Feedback:
The P1 segment is located between the distal basilar artery and the origin of the posterior communicator artery. The P2
segment is located distal to the origin of the posterior communicator artery.

column1:
Which of the following is considered an abnormality that causes turbulence in a vessel?

A: bifurcation
B: tortuosity
C: eccentric change in vessel course
D: myointimal hyperplasia
column2:
Answer: D. myointimal hyperplasia

Feedback:
Abnormal causes of turbulence are acquired. Myointimal hyperplasia occurs with stent or bypass graft placement.
Atherosclerosis formation or bypass grafts are acquired causes of turbulent flow in a vessel. The other choices offered are
normal anatomic variants that can cause turbulence.

column1:
The angular artery is the terminal branch of the

A: Ophthalmic artery
B: Facial artery
C: Anterior cerebral artery
D: Nasal artery
column2:
Answer: B. Facial artery

Feedback:
The angular artery is a terminal branch of the facial artery.

column1:
In most patients, the first branch of the ECA is:

A: the internal thoracic artery
B: the superior thyroidal artery
C: the ophthalmic artery
D: the superficial temporal artery
column2:
Answer: B. the superior thyroidal artery

Feedback:
In most patients, the first branch of the ECA is the superior thyroidal artery. The internal thoracic artery is a branch of the
subclavian artery. The superficial temporal artery is a branch of the ECA but more distally. The ophthalmic artery is a branch of
the ICA.

column1:
Which of the following vessels supply the majority of blood to the cerebellum?

A: ICA and ECA
B: ICA and Vertebral
C: ECA and Basilar
D: Vertebral and Basilar
column2:
Answer: D. Vertebral and Basilar

Feedback:
The cerebellum is located in the posterior fossa of the skull. The vertebral arteries course between the vertebral pedicles to
converge into the basilar artery. Branches from the vertebral and basilar arteries feed the cerebellum and portions of the brain
stem.

column1:
The supraorbital artery is a branch of the ___________________________. Branches of the supraorbital artery connect with branches of the _________________________, which is a branch of the ECA. This is a common collateral pathway from the ECA to the ICA.

A: ICA, ophthalmic artery
B: ophthalmic artery, superficial temporal artery
C: ophthalmic artery, maxillary artery
D: ICA, facial artery
column2:
Answer: B. ophthalmic artery, superficial temporal artery

Feedback:
The supraorbital artery is a branch of the ophthalmic artery. Branches for the supraorbital artery connect with branches of the
superficial temporal artery, which is a branch of the ECA. This is a common collateral pathway from the ECA to the ICA.

column1:
Which of the following describes the normal appearance of a waveform obtained in the middle cerebral artery?

A: low resistance, laminar flow with clear spectral window
B: low resistance flow that varies with patient respiration
C: low resistance with spectral broadening
D: low resistance, low velocity with small flow reversal component in diastole
column2:
Answer: C. low resistance with spectral broadening

Feedback:
The intracranial arteries normally demonstrate low resistance flow due to the low resistance of the brain tissues. Spectral
broadening is normal in these arteries due to the small size of the vessels. A spectral window would not normally be expected.
Diastolic flow reversal would indicate an abnormality causing increased resistance to flow distally.

column1:
The suboccipital window is used in TCD exams to evaluate which of the following arteries?

A: ICA siphon, vertebral and basilar
B: vertebral and basilar
C: ICA siphon
D: MCA and ICA siphon
column2:
Answer: B. vertebral and basilar

Feedback:
The suboccipital window is used to evaluate the vertebral and basilar arteries.

column1:
What blood vessel is used to cause the variations in diastolic flow on the ECA tracing?

A: common carotid artery
B: supraorbital artery
C: superficial temporal artery
D: superficial thyroidal artery
column2:
Answer: C. superficial temporal artery

Feedback:
The temporal tap is performed by lightly tapping the temporal artery located at the soft spot on the anterior aspect of the
temporal bone. The temporal artery is a branch of the ECA and the vibrations from the tapping will be seen within the ECA
waveform. The ICA does not communicate with the temporal artery and no response will be identified from the tapping
maneuver. This technique is used to differentiate the ECA from the ICA.

column1:
Which of the following arteries is normally only an intracranial vessel?

A: superficial temporal artery
B: internal carotid artery
C: vertebral artery
D: basilar artery
column2:
Answer: D. basilar artery

Feedback:
The basilar artery is formed by the convergence of the left and right vertebral arteries inside the posterior aspect of the base of
the skull.

column1:
Using the suboccipital window, the normal basilar and vertebral arteries ______________________.

A: cannot be evaluated using this transcranial window
B: will demonstrate flow away from the probe
C: will demonstrate flow toward the probe
D: will demonstrate bidirectional flow
column2:
Answer: B. will demonstrate flow away from the probe

Feedback:
The suboccipital window is performed by placing the transducer at the base of the skull and angling cephalad into the cranium.
The normal basilar and vertebral arteries will demonstrate flow away from the probe, toward the brain.

column1:
The anterior cerebral artery and its branches feed which of the following lobes of the brain?

A: occipital and frontal
B: occipital and parietal
C: frontal only
D: parietal and frontal
column2:
Answer: D. parietal and frontal

Feedback:
The ACA and its branches feed the parietal and frontal lobes. The middle cerebral artery supplies large portions of the lateral
surfaces of frontal, parietal, and temporal lobes. The vertebral and basilar arteries supply the brain stem, cerebellum, posterior
cerebral cortex. The posterior cerebral arteries originate from the basilar artery to supply the medial temporal and occipital
lobes.

column1:
Which intracranial artery courses within the sylvian fissure?

A: posterior cerebral artery
B: basilar artery
C: anterior cerebral artery
D: middle cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: D. middle cerebral artery

Feedback:
The M2 segment courses within the sylvian fissure toward the lateral aspect of the cranium.

column1:
In most patients the right common carotid artery:

A: originates directly from the aortic arch
B: bifurcates at a lower level in the neck than the left CCA
C: bifurcates at the level if the clavicle
D: is nearly twice the diameter of the left CCA
column2:
Answer: B. bifurcates at a lower level in the neck than the left CCA

Feedback:
Right CCA originates from the innominate artery. Left CCA originates from the aortic arch (second branch from arch). In most
patients the right CCA bifurcates at a lower level than the left CCA.

column1:
Normal brachial pressures should not differ more than ____________ mmHg between arms.

A: 5
B: 10
C: 30
D: 20
column2:
Answer: D. 20

Feedback:
If the brachial pressures differ by more than 20mmHg, a subclavian steal should be suspected.

column1:
The posterior communicating arteries connect the:

A: posterior cerebral artery and middle cerebral artery
B: anterior cerebral artery and posterior cerebral artery
C: basilar and posterior cerebral arteries
D: vertebral and basilar arteries
column2:
Answer: A. posterior cerebral artery and middle cerebral artery

Feedback:
The posterior communicating arteries connect the ipsilateral PCA and MCA.

column1:
During a TCD exam the depth is set to 55mm with a slight anterior angulation while using the temporal window. What vessel will be interrogated and what direction will the flow be moving?

A: middle cerebral artery, toward the transducer
B: anterior cerebral artery, away from the transducer
C: posterior cerebral artery, away from the transducer
D: middle cerebral artery, away from the transducer
column2:
Answer: A. middle cerebral artery, toward the transducer

Feedback:
MCA = 30-60mm depth at temporal window; normally demonstrates flow moving toward the transducer

column1:
Transcranial Doppler most commonly uses which Doppler measurement to evaluate flow in the cerebral vessels?

A: peak systolic velocity
B: time average maximum velocity
C: resistive index
D: end diastolic velocity
column2:
Answer: B. time average maximum velocity

Feedback:
Time average maximum velocity or mean flow velocity is used to assess flow in the cerebral arteries.

column1:
Which of the following is a branch of the ICA?

A: Ophthalmic
B: Temporal
C: Lingual
D: Superior thyroidal
column2:
Answer: A. Ophthalmic

Feedback:
The ophthalmic artery feeds the eye and surrounding structures. This is the only branch of the ICA before it enters the Circle of
Willis. The ECA is responsible for feeding extracranial structures including the face, portions of the throat and neck, tongue and
scalp.
